eras.name: Pre-Columbian Textile Arts
eras.description: The Aztec and Maya civilizations developed extraordinarily sophisticated textile traditions using backstrap looms. Cotton, agave fiber, and featherwork adorned garments that signified social rank, religious status, and regional identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A pre-Columbian Aztec noblewoman wearing an intricately woven cotton huipil with geometric patterns in cochineal red and indigo blue, adorned with quetzal featherwork, standing before a stone temple, warm golden light.

eras.name: Colonial & Mestizo Fusion
eras.description: Spanish colonization introduced European fabrics, silhouettes, and embroidery techniques that merged with indigenous textile traditions. This cross-pollination created hybrid garments that remain iconic in Mexican culture.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A colonial-era Mexican woman in a richly embroidered China Poblana dress with a sequined red and green skirt, draped with a hand-woven silk rebozo, standing in a Puebla courtyard with blue talavera tiles.

eras.name: Revolutionary Identity
eras.description: The Mexican Revolution and the subsequent muralism movement elevated indigenous dress as a symbol of national pride. Frida Kahlo's adoption of Tehuana dress became an international icon of cultural defiance and feminist expression.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A dramatic portrait in the style of Frida Kahlo wearing an elaborate Tehuana headdress with lace huipil grande, heavy jade and coral jewelry, surrounded by tropical flowers, moody cinematic lighting.

eras.name: Golden Age Cinema & Glamour
eras.description: Mexico's golden age of cinema brought national fashion to international audiences. Stars like María Félix and Dolores del Río defined a glamorous Mexican aesthetic that blended Hollywood sophistication with distinctly Mexican sensibility.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1950s Mexican film star in a dramatic black fitted gown with silver filigree jewelry, dark kohl-lined eyes, posed in a Mexico City art deco nightclub, black and white photography with warm tones.

eras.name: Artisanal Revival
eras.description: A renewed appreciation for indigenous textile arts led designers to collaborate with rural artisan communities. This era also sparked global debates about cultural appropriation versus respectful collaboration in fashion.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary fashion editorial featuring a model in a modern structured silhouette dress covered in vibrant Tenango embroidery of birds and flowers, set against the pale pink walls of a Oaxacan street.

eras.name: New Mexican Wave
eras.description: A generation of designers including Carla Fernández, Kris Goyri, and Lydia Lavín are building globally recognized brands that root luxury in indigenous craft. Mexico City has emerged as a creative capital rivaling South American fashion hubs.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Mexico City fashion week runway scene with a model in a sculptural black coat featuring rebozo-inspired draping and Zapotec geometric weaving accents, brutalist concrete architecture backdrop, dramatic spotlight.

textiles.name: Rebozo
textiles.description: A long flat garment woven on a backstrap loom, used as a shawl, baby carrier, and decorative accessory, with intricate tie-dyed (jaspe) patterns.
textiles.technique: Backstrap loom weaving with ikat-style warp dyeing and hand-knotted fringe (rapacejo)

textiles.name: Tenango Embroidery
textiles.description: Colorful narrative embroidery depicting animals, plants, and daily life scenes, originating from Otomí communities in the Tenango de Doria region.
textiles.technique: Freehand satin stitch embroidery on muslin cloth using vibrant cotton threads

textiles.name: Cochineal-Dyed Cotton
textiles.description: A Pre-Columbian dyeing tradition using cochineal insects to produce brilliant crimson, scarlet, and pink hues that were more valuable than gold in colonial trade.
textiles.technique: Natural dye extraction from Dactylopius coccus insects, with mordant variations for color range

textiles.name: Sarape
textiles.description: A brightly striped blanket-like shawl woven from wool or cotton, originally from Saltillo, recognized worldwide as an icon of Mexican craft.
textiles.technique: Treadle loom weaving with concentric diamond center motif and bold stripe patterns

textiles.name: Huipil Brocade
textiles.description: Elaborately brocaded cloth for ceremonial huipiles, with each community having distinctive motifs encoding cosmological and agricultural symbolism.
textiles.technique: Backstrap loom brocade weaving with supplementary weft patterns

climate.fashionImplication: Mexico's diverse climates encourage lightweight cotton and linen in tropical lowlands, layered wool rebozos in highland regions, and broad-brimmed hats for sun protection across the country.

culturalInfluences: Aztec and Maya cosmological symbolism in textile motifs
Frida Kahlo and the Mexican muralism movement
Day of the Dead and religious festival dress traditions
Lucha libre mask culture and pop art
US-Mexico border culture and Chicano identity
Indigenous rights movement and artisan cooperatives

emergingTrends: Neo-Mexican maximalism in color and pattern
Chicano streetwear crossing into high fashion
Digital heritage preservation of endangered weaving techniques
notableEvents: Original Design Festival (Festival de Diseño Original)
